examples.title: Sequential File Read Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates sequential access method - reading records from beginning in order
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SequentialRead(fileName)
  OPEN fileName FOR READING
  WHILE NOT EOF(fileName) DO
    READ record FROM fileName
    PROCESS record
  END WHILE
  CLOSE fileName
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This algorithm processes records in sequential order. The access pointer starts at the beginning and moves forward only. For large files where only specific records are needed, this method becomes inefficient as all preceding records must be read first.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is total records, or O(k) to read k records from start
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only one record in buffer at a time

examples.title: Direct Access Read Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates direct access method using relative record numbers
examples.code:
PROCEDURE DirectRead(fileName, RRN)
  RECORD_SIZE ← 100  // bytes
  baseAddress ← 0
  
  // Calculate physical address
  offset ← (RRN - 1) * RECORD_SIZE
  physicalAddress ← baseAddress + offset
  
  // Seek directly to position and read
  SEEK fileName TO physicalAddress
  READ record FROM fileName
  
  RETURN record
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Direct access calculates the exact physical location of a record using its Relative Record Number (RRN). The formula (RRN-1) × record_size gives the byte offset from the file start. This provides O(1) access time as no searching is required.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - constant time to calculate address and read

examples.title: Simple Hash Function Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates hash-based access with collision handling using linear probing
examples.explanation: This demonstrates hash-based direct access. The hash function maps keys to bucket addresses. When collisions occur (two keys hash to same address), linear probing finds the next available slot. This provides O(1) average-case access time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average case, O(n) worst case
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storage, O(1) for search operation

examples.title: Indexed Sequential Access Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates ISAM random access through hierarchical indexes
examples.explanation: ISAM provides fast random access through a hierarchical index structure. The primary index points to track indexes, which point to data blocks. Each level narrows the search space, resulting in O(log n) complexity. For sequential access, records can be read sequentially from the sorted data file or by following index chains.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) for index search + O(b) for block search where b is block size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for search, O(m) where m is index levels loaded
